title: Tuna Caesar with Butternut Squash & Parmesan Crisps
summary: Zingy lemon yoghurt, roast squash, and seared tuna come together in this high-protein bowl topped with crispy Parmesan shards. A serious bit of meal prep.

method.description: Chop the butternut squash into 5cm pieces, chuck onto a baking tray with half the olive oil, season with salt and black pepper, then roast for 20 mins or until tender and starting to colour.

Peel and finely grate the garlic into a mixing bowl. Mash the anchovies and garlic together using a fork to form a paste. Zest over half the lemon (save the rest for later), then squeeze in the juice. Add the yoghurt, Worcestershire sauce, Dijon mustard and plenty of black pepper. Whisk together, loosening with splashes of cold water until it’s as runny as you like.

Heat a nonstick frying pan over a medium heat. Finely grate the Parmesan directly into the pan in an even layer and cook until bubbling. Remove from the heat, let it cool for a couple of minutes, then gently lift the Parmesan crisp out. Once cool, snap into shards.
Strip the cavolo nero leaves from their stems and finely shred the leaves. Toss into a bowl with a pinch of salt and the remaining lemon juice. Scrunch it all together with your hands to soften.

Brush the tuna steaks with the remaining oil and season with salt and black pepper. Heat a large nonstick frying pan over a medium-high heat, then cook the tuna for 2 mins per side for a pink centre. Transfer to a plate to rest, then sprinkle over the reserved lemon zest.

Toss the roasted squash and lemony dressing through the cavolo nero, then divide between Tupperware containers. Slice the tuna and lay it on top, finishing with the Parmesan crisps. Box up and pop in the fridge, ready for when you need it. These keep for 3 days in the fridge.

chefs.name: Anna Helm Baxter
chefs.summary: Anna Helm Baxter is a cookbook author, food stylist, and food producer at Mob. Having spent most of her life in the US, she loves potlucks, tailgates, and a good Thanksgiving spread. As a working mum of three, she gets the dinner struggle, which is why she’s all about recipes that are joyful, healthy, and totally doable. And if all else fails she always has cookies stashed in the freezer.
